Simply I was hooked, and I admit I'm a big admirer of the original play Hamlet. The film does start off slowly, but once it gets going, you'll be gripped. It's hard to single out a single item alone that sold this film to me, and I really don't want to give anything away, as really this is a film has some shocks. When you note that most films portraying Shakespeare as rose-tinted & over romanticised, this is a surprise.

It's grim at many times, but the dark heart helps to explain Hamlet, and I was left aghast seeing the original Hamlet in a very new light. It was some achievement, and a notable point was just how unlikeable that our lead Shakespeare was made to be through large parts of the film when he's often only seen as a hero.

The acting by the cast was excellent, and you've got to hand it to Paul Mescal who really does pull a rabbit out of the hat after the absolutely rotten Gladiator II movie. Jessie Buckley as his wife ('Agnes') was brilliant, and the awards deserve to pile up for both of them, albeit they will be fighting hard against 'One Battle After Another'.

I can't recommend this highly enough, please go out to watch this. A wonderfully written, directed & acted movie.

raw_payload.author: mwdyam
raw_payload.content: I didn't go into this film expecting much and it was such a wonderful experience watching the story unfold and Jessie buckley's raw and emotional performance. I don't normally engage emotionally with movies but this one sucked me right in. The film was so good I forgive Paul Mescal for Carmen and Gladiator II. He was terrific in this film. Hopefully he will continue to pick quality projects from now on. Jessie Buckley is wonderful in everything she does.

raw_payload.author: harold-819-540531
raw_payload.content: I don't think I would recommend seeing Hamnet. The acting was very good, but the movie seemed flat to me. Everything had the same intensity, as if the writer and director were trying to make every scene as intense as possible, rather than focusing on telling an interesting story. For me, it is a movie where everything is left to the viewer's interpretation.

raw_payload.author: willisthetall
raw_payload.content:
What is grief, if not love persevering?

Agnes and William's relationship is portrayed with care and authenticity. Initially, it is a burning and unlikely romance between a woman connected to nature and an indebted teacher (it even recalls the classical love stories of the period). Throughout the first act, it develops into a solid marriage, having good moments (with the children, mainly) and some dilemmas (when William has to go to London, leaving the family behind), exactly like a real marriage. It all, along with the huge chemistry between Mescal and Buckley and excellent dialogues, makes their relationship a strong example of the power of writing and acting.

It is also important to highlight that Agnes and William are also very well-developed by themselves, they don't need that relationship to be good characters. Agnes's connection with nature is deeply shown through her knowledge about plants, her decision of having a baby in the forest, camera angles - I would like to highlight also the moments the camera focuses on the entrance of a cave, serving as a symbol that shows that the nature is looking back at the characters -, small details in the characterization, etc. William is not so deeply presented (he's not the main character, after all), but his passion for stories, his love to Agnes and their children and his disdain for his father are well showed.

Agnes and William's family are well-developed. The movie shows their routine, games and cheerful moments, creating an awesome portrayal of a solid and happy family... and that is what makes Hamnet's death so hard to see, the film made the audience love they all and, suddenly, robbed their happiness with the plague. All this work of Chloé Zhao to make us care about Agnes and William and, after, their family created a feeling of "collective mourning".

The last great thing in this movie is exactly what makes it a 10/10 movie: it has an incredible portrayal of the mourning. When Hamnet dies, the film stops: who we saw happy some minutes before are now suffering, arguing and (even with a big loss) having to go on with their lives. Agnes and William didn't "forget" Hamnet at the end of the third act, they, as any real couple should do when something like that happens, just learned how to handle his death - the scene on the theater shows it perfectly, William clearly wrote the play 'Hamlet' as a way to digest better his loss.

This movie tells a beautiful and timeless story. I am sure this is a movie that will become a classic very, very soon. Just a skilled director like Chloé Zhao would be able to create a movie the viewer will take for his life. Honestly, I am very excited to see her future works.

raw_payload.author: Arthouse_dork
raw_payload.content:
I don't usually go for period dramas, but I'm so glad I caught Hamnet in theaters. The film eschews flashy montages for a grounded, realistic visual style, using static shots that give the actors room to breathe.

Jessie Buckley is a national treasure and truly one of the best of her generation; her performance is raw, natural, and utterly captivating. While Paul Mescal offers great support and young Jacobi Jupe proves he's a rising star to watch, this is Buckley's show.

The third act loses its momentum briefly, and while the film as a whole might not be "revolutionary," the emotional journey and powerful soundtrack make it a strong recommendation.

raw_payload.author: goodmudra
raw_payload.content:
Hamnet, directed by Chloé Zhao, is a quiet yet profoundly affecting meditation on grief, love, and loss, told unmistakably through the female experience. From the very beginning, we are reminded that Hamlet and Hamnet are interchangeable, an idea that quietly frames the film and hints at how deeply personal loss becomes transformed into art.

Jessie Buckley delivers a remarkable performance as Agnes, William Shakespeare's woodsy wife, grounding the film in maternal strength, intuition, and emotional intelligence, and shifting the focus away from Shakespeare's public success to the inner life of the woman behind his creation.

Zhao's restrained direction and lyrical cinematography immerse the viewer in the textures and rhythms of 15th-century life. The film unfolds slowly and sensorially, inviting us to listen, observe, and feel rather than rush toward narrative resolution. Themes of childbirth, emotional absence, and the loss of a child are portrayed with an intimacy that feels embodied rather than observed, giving the film particular resonance for female viewers.

The final act is haunting and deeply moving, subtly weaving personal experiences of grief with the transformative power of art. Scenes inspired by Hamlet do not demand literal understanding, but instead work on an emotional level, allowing feeling, memory and interpretation to take precedence over explanation. Hildur Guðnadóttir's evocative score acts as an emotional anchor throughout, offering something tangible to hold onto during moments of overwhelming loss.

More than a historical drama, Hamnet is a reflective exploration of how love and grief endure, and how creativity can help us survive what feels unbearable. It lingers long after the film ends, not because of spectacle, but because of its honesty, restraint, and emotional depth.

raw_payload.author: TVwatchr2024
raw_payload.content:
There were many times when the dialogue was indistinct, muffled and totally unintelligible. Mostly it was the character Agnes, sometimes her husband.

I thought it may have been the particular venue where I viewed Hamnet, wishing to give the film the benefit of the doubt.

I have, however, just been part of a conversation with other Book Club members where this matter was discussed at great length. Most members had the same issue.

Many say they will have to wait for the movie to be streamed so they can view with CC.

raw_payload.author: mysterymoviegoer
raw_payload.content: A fascinating story becomes a so-so movie. What's off? A lot. Mescal's Shakespeare doesn't suggest a man with a great interest in the theater or writing. In fact he's rather dull. We are told things about life in Stratford but don't always see them. The direction is uneven. It seems to lose focus at times. What am I supposed to be noticing here. Scenes containing two live birth go on and on. Some scenes sag. The print I saw in a theater was not very sharp. Why does Ann (Agnes) laugh at the end. The writing gets very pedestrian at times. Some scenes plod along. Tighter editing would have helped. Also tighter continuity. The premise here to explain Hamlet is potentially intriguing but this telling doesn't do it justice. It's not terrible but it's not very good either.

raw_payload.author: RyanM-350
raw_payload.content:
The past two years has largely been one of the most barren periods for film in the past five decades. I have become inured to the pallid offerings, ripping off one review after another for the middling to disastrous field of films. Confronted with a truly great cinematic experience leaves me struggling to find the proper words.

Even as the opening scenes of Hamnet demonstrate the besotted bard tongue tied and twisted, the words must come. Chloé Zhao has fashioned a monumental masterpiece and it needs to be shouted from the mountaintops.

Released into a world of hectoring hate, Hamnet doesn't have sides. Its themes of renewal, forgiveness, despair and community are universal and eternal. A plot summary is unerringly simple yet the story culminates in a chaotically cathartic crescendo that is both heart rending and uplifting.

Hamnet stars the suddenly ubiquitous and soon to be Oscar winner Jessie Buckley as Agnes. Her performance is a masterwork of hope piercing grief, sustaining spousal forbearance and maternal love which crosses the earthly threshold.

Paul Mescal, as William Shakespeare, takes on the quieter more internalized role. For a brief moment, after Hamnet's bravura central sequence, I wondered if the film could conclude with the same type of grace. Mescal's stoic and haunting portrayal of desolation sets the tone for the film's final act. His is the whisper to Buckley's scream and they pair powerfully.

With an assist from the most beguiling words ever written, Zhao and Maggie O'Farrell's screenplay fuses the earthly with the divine. The direction creates a visual cipher representing the nexus between the here and now and the ever after.

In the hands of a lesser artist that slap lands, the father's villainy amplifies and Agnes' dialogue sounds like the utterances of a 21st century feminist. Hamnet is too high minded to bring that kind of low. Instead the tone is warm and welcoming with ample stylistic flourishes

If the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences has any remaining decency it will award Hamnet Best Picture. The film is light and ethereal and lyrical with a beautiful soul. The current leading contender is base and unambiguous and mean while appealing to society's lower instincts. I won't get my hopes up. Hamnet is the proof I craved, however, these past two years. Great films still exist.

The rest is silence.

raw_payload.author: PotassiumMan
raw_payload.content:
How did William Shakespeare's motivation for writing Hamlet come to be? That's what this incredibly moving film tackles with conviction and painstaking effort. As the Bard's artistic career starts to materialize in London, his wife Agnes raises their children. Agnes, rebellious and thoughtful, has defied her repressive household by choosing the Latin tutor. Will has started to overpower his degenerate father and chart his own path. Their family life seems perfect until one of their children abruptly dies from a illness caused by a contagious plague. From there, Agnes and Will start to diverge.

Jessie Buckley is incandescent as Agnes. This story is as much about her as it is about him. Most of the narrative unfolds through her heartbreak and resilience. Will himself turns out to be something of a supporting perspective. Paul Mescal is splendid as the young Shakespeare who is slowly finding his voice, and whose artistic focus starts to become perfectionistic and ferocious. It is only toward the end that we see how these two vantage points coalesce into one truly mesmerizing whole. Supporting performances from Emily Watson and Joe Alwyn are another strong suit.

Themes of mourning the loss of a child and slowly evolving artistic creation are woven together. It's sort of an amalgamation of "Rabbit Hole" and "Shakespeare in Love" and with Chloe Zhao's deliberate and challenging approach, it becomes a portrayal that is deeply memorable and spectacular. The climax delivers in spades and will stay with you long afterward. Recommended to the highest degree.

raw_payload.author: JohnG_43
raw_payload.content:
First of all, this film is breathtaking. From the opening shot to the final one, every moment is visually captivating. There's no in-your-face intense color grading or blatantly "cinematic" imagery- just wide angle shots which transport you to sixteenth-century England, and intimate close-up shots which make you share in the characters' experiences. The use of sound is masterful as well- some scenes are nearly silent, letting you hear the characters breathe, whereas other scenes are filled with music that amplifies the emotional experience tenfold. I honestly cannot think of another film which so perfectly balances a beautiful soundtrack with powerful silence. On top of that, the acting is brilliant; even the little children seem to capture some deep level of human emotion.

Of course, like all movies, the most important aspect of this work is not its technical achievement, but rather its reflection of the human experience. I will admit that that I cried on multiple occasions during the screening, and when the end credits finally rolled in, I couldn't contain myself from sobbing loudly (my sincere apologies to the other people in the theater). Admittedly, Hamnet can be painful to watch. Much of the film is focused on grief and loss. But, just like Shakespeare himself and his wife Agnes, we are ultimately reminded that art comforts us in our pain and stories make our loved ones immortal. We are reminded that, in spite of all its suffering, life is beautiful, and we can never take it for granted. The message is timeless and powerful; it will stick with you after you leave the theater.

Chloe Zhao, I salute you.

raw_payload.author: tommy5677
raw_payload.content:
This film arrives with the force of a cultural event, the kind that instantly recalibrates expectations for what cinema can achieve. From its opening moments to its devastating final frame, it commands total attention and leaves a lasting emotional imprint. This is not merely a great movie-it feels like a defining work of its era.

The performances are nothing short of stupendous, operating at a level of precision and emotional truth that is rarely seen. Every actor inhabits their role so completely that the line between performance and lived experience dissolves. The smallest gestures carry seismic weight, and the silences speak as loudly as the dialogue. This is acting that doesn't ask for attention; it demands it.

As a drama, the film is astonishingly intense, sustaining a level of emotional pressure that never relents yet never feels manipulative. Scene after scene lands with clarity and force, building toward moments that are as harrowing as they are cathartic. The emotional journey is immersive and consuming, making it one of the most powerful dramatic experiences imaginable.

The direction demonstrates absolute command of tone, pacing, and visual storytelling. Every creative choice feels purposeful, from the framing of shots to the rhythm of the edit. The craftsmanship amplifies the performances rather than overshadowing them, resulting in a unified vision that feels both meticulous and deeply human.

This film has all the hallmarks of an Oscar contender, not as a matter of hype but of inevitability. Its ambition is matched by its execution, and its emotional impact lingers long after the credits roll. Awards recognition would feel less like celebration and more like acknowledgment of something undeniable.

By the time it ends, the film leaves you shaken, reflective, and profoundly moved. It stands as a towering achievement in dramatic storytelling and a reminder of cinema's unique power to reach into the deepest places of the human experience.

raw_payload.author: mezzaitaliana-89131
raw_payload.content:
The reviews will differ if people have or haven't read Maggie O'Farrells novel Hamnet.

The novel is a reimagining of Agnes/Anne and William Shakespear the budding playwright.

In the novel Agnes is a well known healer in her area. Which in the 16th century could be dangerous. This wasn't portrayed. The film mainly focuses on their meeting, childbirth and grief.

It was a well made film with a great a performance from Jessie Buckley. But I found it very melodramatic compared to the book.

raw_payload.author: jonbower
raw_payload.content: Hamnet is an astonishing movie. The story is simple but the acting, cinematography and music are perfect. The result is an emotional wringer that satisfies the soul. The people, their relationships and their growth more than makes up for the measured pace. It is the opposite of the modern tent pole movie, harkening back to the days of adult drama for adults.

raw_payload.author: JustinB-8626
raw_payload.content: This is not a film for anyone expecting to learn about previously unknown aspects of Shakespeare's personal and private life. It is pure speculation from start to finish, and relies on the fact that we know so little about the man himself that 'biographers' and film makers can say anything they like without fear of evidence-based contradiction. To understand the mind of Shakespeare and the workings of his poetic imagination, we have his output and ONLY his output. Read it, read it again, and read it closely, thoroughly and with understanding. There are no shortcuts, but your own inner life, imagination and language will be enriched beyond measure.

raw_payload.author: chong_an
raw_payload.content:
This is a story of the life of William Shakespeare and his family in the Stratford environs, based on a few known facts. The time period is from Shakespeare meeting his wife, to the opening of Hamlet in London. A lot of time is spent on the drama of home life, including that with some of the in-laws. Not being much use locally other than as a Latin tutor, Shakepeare leaves for London. The desire for his family to join him is put off with various excuses, until a successful Shakespeare buys his marital family the biggest house in town.

Being away much on business, with occasional visits home, Shakespeare misses the birth, death, and funeral of his only son Hamnet (a.k.a. Hamlet). Instead, he uses Hamnet's name for the title and lead character in his tragic play, as ghosts of father and son abound.

The mostly rural scenery has a washed-out coloring, seeming to indicate a historical story (similar to Nuremberg), accentuated by dim or available-light-only photography.

Because the story is so much concentrated in the family life in the Stratford area, it is fitting that the credits list his wife first, not Shakespeare. Shakespeare may have played sword-fighting with his son, inspiring the closing scene of Hamlet. There are quotes from Shakespearean plays, as the (screen)writer may be stretching to connect Shakespeare's plays with his family. His wife, being a witch and medicine woman, may have inspired the witches of Macbeth.

The problem, I find, is that if you are not a fan of Shakespeare, and do not know his work reasonably well, you lose a significant part of the plot. For films currently playing, I have recommended Rental Family for those interested in Japanese culture, and Left Hand Girl for those interested in Taipei life. However, does anyone care about life in rural 16th century England, even if there was a major historical figure in it? This I why I can only give this film a rating of 7, as it is not for everyone.

raw_payload.author: ferguson-6
raw_payload.content:
Greetings again from the darkness. Watching so many movies each year usually raises my defense mode against cinematic emotional manipulation. However, all hope is not lost for me as the best filmmakers and talented writers and top notch actors can still get to me, as evidenced by this latest from writer-director Chloe Zhao (two Oscars for NOMADLAND, 2020; plus the underappreciated THE RIDER, 2017) and novelist-screenwriter Maggie O'Farrell (her 2020 novel is the source material).

The actors who manage to elevate this extraordinary work to new heights are two of the best actors working today: Jessie Buckley (highly recommend WILD ROSE, 2018) and Paul Mescal (AFTERSUN, 2022). Mr. Mescal plays Will Shakespeare as a tutor at a rural school. He aspires to be a writer (spoiler alert: he succeeds). Ms. Buckley plays Agnes, a local who is labeled as a child of the forest witch, and she actually possesses the spirit of nature and healing. The meet-cute of Will and Agnes is anything but. Spotting Agnes out the window as she carries her pet hawk back to the barn, Will abandons his students and rushes outside to meet her. Yada-Yada ... she's pregnant ... the families are not happy ... the young couple begins their life together.

Even in the late 16th century, succeeding as a playwright was no easy task. Will often took leave from his family to travel to London for work. A second pregnancy for Agnes bore twins, including daughter Judith who was weakened, and son Hamnet. All in all, this was a close-knit family who experienced joy together despite the challenges - quite a contrast from Will's family where his dad called him "useless".

It should be noted that this does not come across as the story of the great William Shakespeare. Instead, it's the story of a family whose heart is Agnes, and it's told from her perspective. We are informed that names of the time often had various spellings, so you might be more familiar with Agnes as Anne Hathaway, and son Hamnet as Hamlet, the inspiration for one of Shakespeare's greatest works. To say more on this would be a spoiler I'm unwilling to provide. You should know that son Hamnet is played by Jacobi Jupe (PETER PAN & WENDY, 2023) and the stage character Hamlet is played by his real-life brother Noah Jupe (FORD V FERRARI, 2019). Emily Watson and Joe Alwyn also have supporting roles.

To be or not to be ... a tearjerker ... is no question. Director Zhao is a master and Mr. Mescal and especially Ms. Buckley cut right to the heart of a story that will have quite an impact on most every viewer. Grief is such a powerful emotion, and the contrast in how a couple handles it ... even a couple deeply in love ... makes for a story and a film that is surely one of the year's best.

raw_payload.author: NeilEv
raw_payload.content:
One of the saddest films of recent years, Hamnet dramatizes the grief of Will (William Shakespeare) and his wife Agnes (Anne Hathaway) in 16th century England. Shot almost entirely in available light, the film's candlelit interiors and shadowed exteriors lend authenticity, but obscure the visual elements. In addition, listening to Elizabethan dialogue, makes the movie difficult to follow without subtitles.

The narrative is unflinching. Agnes gives birth in the forest without medical aid, their falcon dies early, and the bubonic plague claims their only son, Hamnet. Will, torn between family and career, spends much of his time in London writing and rehearsing. His redemption arrives when Agnes witnesses his play The Tragedy of Hamlet and recognizes it as a tribute to their lost child, underscoring the interchangeability of Hamnet and Hamlet in that era.

Though the film feels like deliberate Oscar bait, echoing Shakespeare in Love with a tragic twist, it succeeds in portraying raw sorrow and artistic transformation. A challenging but poignant watch.

raw_payload.author: hoernkeem
raw_payload.content:
Eric's Grade: A 'Hamnet' is a 2025 drama directed by Chloe Zhao from a screenplay by Zhao and Maggie O'Farrell based on O'Farrell's 2020 novel of the same name which is a fictionalized account of the marriage of William Shakespeare and Agnes Hathaway. The film stars Paul Mescal and Jessie Buckley with supporting roles for Emily Watson and Joe Alwyn. It debuted at the Telluride Film Festival in August 2025, and was released in theaters in November. 'Hamnet' has only done modest box office business, but has received critical acclaim landing on numerous end of year best lists including being named a top ten film of 2025 by the American Film Institute.

Interestingly we know that Shakespeare had a son named Hamnet, and wrote a tragedy entitled Hamlet. An opening statement remarks during that time in England Hamnet and Hamlet were interchangeable names. Mescal stars as William Shakespeare who is tutoring some local village kids in Stratford to pay off his father's debt. At one of these sessions Will spies a woman outdoors with a falcon. He is quite taken with her, and learns her name is Agnes. She is more of a pagan, free spirit and spends time in the forest communing with nature which inspires rumors about her. Will and Agnes, played by Buckley, quickly become intimate with Agnes getting pregnant. The two wed in a forest ceremony although both families frown at the union. Agnes gives birth to a child in the forest. When William chafes trying to apprentice for his father Agnes realizes he must go off to London to pursue his dream of the theater leaving his wife and child behind. We are not privy to much of what transpires in London with Will but later Agnes gives birth two twins Hamnet and Judith. When Will is home he is a loving doting father, but he goes back to London frequently as he gains popularity, and works even more. Judith becomes ill, contracting the plague. Agnes always thought she would die with only two children. When Will returns he finds Judith fine, but Hamnet is dead. The despair between Will and Agnes is palpable, and Will flees to London again. When Agnes finds out Will has created a play called 'Hamlet' she finally journeys to London to see for herself what her husband had crafted in his grief.

I knew basically what I was getting into watching 'Hamnet'. I knew that Shakespeare had a son named Hamnet who died young. This is a fictionalized account rather than historical record as scholars do not think the play was inspired by the death of his son although this story draws that direct connection. I knew basically this was going to be Shakespeare meeting his wife, having kids, Hamnet dying, and 'Hamlet' gets written, and for the most part that is exactly how this story progresses. I was shocked at how the deft script is, and how these amazing actors draw you in. The screenplay does an a stellar job of not lingering on nonessential issues that would weigh down the narrative. Early on I was wondering if there was going to be a lot wrapped up in the conflict of the families fighting against this union, but that never develops because it isn't necessary. We instead focus on exactly the things we need to. Likewise we stay away from Will building his career as a playwright in London only seeing a brief snippet of him getting frustrated with his actors when they are rehearsing 'Hamlet'. Mescal who I did not like at all in 'Gladiator II' perfectly fits in this role of Shakespeare, and Buckley is wonderful playing Agnes with a passionate ferocity. Another interesting aspect to the narrative is you think a lot would be wrapped up in Will and Agnes' love for each other, but it is much more about grief, and how they come to terms with that grief separately. The film is shot exceptionally well with great visuals and cinematography. The finale to 'Hamnet' is simple as we watch the play 'Hamlet' being staged with Will playing Hamlet's father's ghost. We watch the performance as well as Agnes' reaction, and the audience around her culminating in the most emotional final scene I saw in a movie in 2025. I don't even know how to describe it because a simple description does not quite convey all that those final images evoke. That last sequence does a lot of heavy lifting, but the whole movie is a gorgeous, superbly acted emotionally cathartic production that squarely places 'Hamnet' as one of the best films of 2025.

raw_payload.author: li0904426
raw_payload.content:
The movie "Hamnet" is a historical drama that reimagines the lives of William Shakespeare's family, with a particular focus on his wife and their son, Hamnet.

Director Chloé Zhao created a beautiful film with a good story, but the dialogues are weak, confusing, and unconvincing. One moment it feels like we're in the 16th century, and the next, it's the 20th century. The characters also seem inconsistent in their behavior.

What stands out in the story is how author Maggie O'Farrell uses imagination to link the creation of Hamlet with Shakespeare's personal grief over the loss of a family member. What's particularly impressive is how Hamlet serves as a perfect vehicle for illustrating how art can transform pain into a means of healing and redemption.

Jessie Buckley delivers an excellent performance as Shakespeare's wife, especially considering the challenge of delivering dialogues that span two distinct eras.

raw_payload.author: jpcronen
raw_payload.content: The events portrayed and the emotional impact on the characters represented are painful and traumatic. IMHO Chloe Z hits all the right notes...this is a film of deep emotional maturity. Rarely does modern film mine those depths of pain, suffering and healing. The film represents the paradox of life in all its forms. Worth seeing, to appreciate that film in its most basic form can still stir deep emotions and tap into the spirit of the human condition. A triumph 10/10.

raw_payload.author: Ayupgeeza
raw_payload.content:
Hamnet is divided into two unequal halves: the first is set in Stratford and tells the story of Shakespeare, his wife and family and their life there; the second is set around a performance of the play Hamlet in the Globe Theatre in London.

The first half is a mostly visual experience: lingering shots of forests and trees and suchlike. There is little dialogue, and much of that is mumbled, stumbled over, talked over or inaudible. Presumably the aim was to present a contrast with the second part of the film, but it's a curious choice to make a film about Shakespeare in which language is treated as incidental, almost irrelevant. Even Shakespeare himself struggles to get his words out, and when he does, those words are barely worth waiting for. It's almost insulting to Shakespeare to hear him speak in meaningless platitudes even in the most emotional moments of his life, as he does here. The result is a very slow, often tedious, frequently pretentious first half of the film; for all the beauty on screen, it's a joyless and often difficult viewing experience.

The glue holding this first half together is Jessie Buckley. Paul Mescal's Shakespeare is almost a part-player in this first half, which focuses on Buckley's character. Without much of a script, Buckley seems at times to be carrying on through force of will alone, but she is nevertheless utterly captivating, and manages to keep the audience's attention from wandering too far. She is ably supported in this by extraordinary performances from her young castmates portraying her children, particularly Jacobi Jupe, whose portrayal of Hamnet is one of the finest performances by a child actor that I've ever seen.

If the film had continued in that vein it would have been a singular disappointment. However, when the setting shifts to the Globe Theatre, the film finally draws these emotional threads together and delivers a genuinely staggering denouement. It is Shakespeare communicating his grief at the death of his son through his play 'Hamlet', and his wife's realisation of the depth of feeling of her husband. It is an extraordinarily powerful weaponisation of Shakespeare's verse, and the film does a truly incredible job of conveying the emotions prevalent in one of Shakespeare's most tortuous tragedies.

The contrast between the two halves of this film could not be more stark. In the first, the audience at the screening I attended was restless, fidgety and distracted; by the end of the second I doubt there was a dry eye in the house.

raw_payload.author: csm-78119

raw_payload.author: 210west
raw_payload.content:
Maggie O'Farrell, author of the novel, seems like a wise and lovely lady, and I'm happy for her that her book has received such a handsome production. And Jessie Buckley is usually a marvelous actress.

Here, though, as Mrs. Will Shakespeare, when not wandering around Stratford's woods pretending to be a sort of all-knowing female nature spirit, she spends a lot of time screaming her lungs out and -- when in London to see what her husband has been working on -- a lot of time gazing around like a goggle-eyed child.

The performance of "Hamlet" she witnesses at the end of the movie has got to be the dullest and least impressive I've ever seen, even if the ghost is played by Shakespeare himself. The onstage acting is uninspired, indeed deadly dull, the sort of thing that would have bored the groundlings stiff rather than hold them spellbound (as the movie would have you believe). The play's staging is even more lackluster and amateurish. (I speak as a former groundling at the restored Globe, where the performance I attended seemed both more powerful and more authentic.)

As one critic has pointed out, the painted sets on the movie's "stage" depict, inappropriately, a verdant forest, clearly to connect us moviegoers with the Stratford woodland where so much of the previous action has taken place -- yet there's no forest in "Hamlet," which takes place in a castle!

And I just do NOT believe, if Anne Hathaway had actually shown up at a performance of "Hamlet," that she'd have screamed histrionically at the actors on stage, "DON'T YOU DARE PRONOUNCE MY SON'S NAME!" That's an awfully implausible movie-ish gesture that, in real life, might have deservedly gotten her thrown out of the theater.

Finally, almost every writer I've ever known has been someone who loves to hear the sound of his or her own voice and won't stop talking -- whereas the movie's Will Shakespeare seems a man of improbably few words, closer to a brawny inarticulate hunk than a future author.

raw_payload.author: hrssagas
raw_payload.content:
The film is mostly slow, driven by its theatrical style and outstanding performances, yet above all by the brilliance of the direction and the inventive use of cinematography.

The story itself is not particularly remarkable, but what is done with it is. The camera's creative use expands the space, opening portals as in theater itself.

Forests become mystical, the exit from a stage turns into an insurmountable gate, and a house transforms into absence. All of this occurs without losing the narrative focus.

It is a work that offers something for everyone, whether they appreciate subtext, straightforward narratives, or symbolism.

However, the theatrical language, combined with the pacing, may distance some viewers.

raw_payload.author: DrGadimov

First act sweet love story, second slower (wondered where going) but necessary, third pure gold on stage. Themes of grief turning to art, family, loss. raw_payload.author: TuesdayButterfly
raw_payload.content:
I'll admit I was confused at first. I wasn't sure where the story was starting in Shakespeare's life. I also thought his wife's name was Anne Hathaway, so when he called her something different, I was thrown off. Don't let that distract you if you think you know a bit about Shakespeare's history. Just listen and be patient. I also found it interesting that they didn't say his name until the end of the film. Because of that, the relationships at the beginning took a moment to settle in.

I think it's best to go in not expecting to see Shakespeare's life as you know it. Forget everything you think you know and give the film your full attention, almost like a voyeur stepping into this world.

I sat in the front row of the theater. I saw what language can do, how it is understood, and how its meaning can change. The images carried in the actors' voices were incredibly clear. I saw new facets of Hamlet that I never knew existed. This film taught me so much about language, perception, love, and will, and how art can transcend the human condition. That may sound cliché, but this movie is proof that many clichés exist because they are true.

I cried at the end. Max Richter is a genius, and Chloé Zhao's direction is outstanding.

I've seen this and One Battle After Another, and if I'm being honest and objective, I believe this will win Best Picture. OBAA is a story of this time, of this moment. Hamnet is timeless. This film reminds us why art and storytelling are essential to our survival as an intelligent species, and how memory and language can be shared and remain just as relevant today as they were 400 years ago.

I was deeply moved, humbled, and changed by this film. Thank you for making it. Thank you for inspiring me. Thank you for reminding me to keep going and keep creating.

raw_payload.author: cdjh-81125
raw_payload.content:
Hamnet didn't have much on paper that got me overly excited if I'm being honest with myself. Chloe Zhao is a filmmaker I've found very cold up until now and even as someone who has a little bit of a soft sport for Oscar bait the trailers just didn't look like much more than that to me. It was the promise of strong technical crafts and great performances that peaked my interest and the film delivered on those fronts as well as some that I didn't expect it to.

Jessie Buckley deserves every bit of excitement she's generating with her performance in this movie because it's almost sickening just how realistic it feels. She has to portray a lot of humanity, romantic chemistry, motherly love as well as an agonising amount of grief and she channels all of it perfectly, and I mean perfectly. It's one of these performances in which it's hard to pick a best scene but a birthing scene and the scene in which the actual titular tragedy takes place stand out as particular highlights. If and when she wins the Oscar for this movie it will be 100% deserving. Paul Mescal is also phenomenal in how restrained his performance is and the moments in which he breaks down are all the more powerful as a result. He has great chemistry with Jessie Buckley from the outset and it almost feels as if the foundation of the movie are the scenes in which they are going back and forth and they're best material that the movie has to offer.

Although I have my misgivings about Chloe Zhao as a filmmaker I can't deny her ability to deliver very striking visuals and Hamnet is no different. She seems to take every opportunity to tell the story visually and there are more than a few images that genuinely shook me. The cinematography is gorgeous and the score is very effecting so on a performance and technical level I have no complaints. I think it is just the story itself and some of the writing decisions that hold things back for me. The film is paced in a very patient way but I'd be lying if I didn't say it frequently come across as meandering to me.

I think that's because the first act in particular plays out like a standard period piece romance that felt quite standard to me and it almost felt as if a lot of the key moments in the relationship were being skimmed over. When the film transitions into being more of a family drama things picked up but I wouldn't say I was ever as engaged as I wanted to be. The film has a lot of individually great scenes but I wanted a bit more from it as an actual narrative. I think the third act also suffers from a need to overly explain itself with dialogue rather than just letting Jessie Buckley emote her characters feelings in more restrained way. Having said that though the film absolutely ends on the strongest note imaginable with its best scene that perfectly delivers its overall themes in the most effective way.

I haven't always been able to get too invested in Shakespeare adaptations but Hamnet is absolutely one of the better ones I've seen. It's anchored by an incredible Jessie Buckley performance and a phenomenal Paul Mescal with some absolutely striking visuals. I wish I could have gotten more invested in the story but maybe do have to be honest and say that it is likely that for the type of movie this is it was only going to be able to do so much for me.

raw_payload.author: ProfWinerReviews
raw_payload.content:
The story is simple. Will and Agnes Shakespeare have three children including twins Hamnet and Judith while living in Stratford-on-Avon. Will travels a lot to London as he becomes involved with the world of theater while Agnes takes care of the household. Hamnet dies tragically of the plague at the age of 11 while Will is away. Agnes has difficulty forgiving Will who leaves again to write a new play which turns out to be Hamlet. Agnes appears in Stratford for the premiere of Hamlet where Will is playing the spirit of Hamlet's deceased father.

However, the plot does not do justice to the emotional impact of the film. Jessie Buckley does an amazing job as Agnes. Her emotional outburst when Hamnet dies just tears at your heart. Although Buckley is only 36, it is perhaps a career-defining role. She is perfect as a girl of the forest, a free spirit, who is tough enough to raise three children virtually without a husband but also emotionally vulnerable. As Will, Paul Mescal continues his series of affecting roles (Aftersun, The History of Sound) that further demonstrate his acting chops (not his muscles in this one as in Gladiator II). All the child actors are amazing as is the dependable Emily Watson as Will's mother Mary. The photography of the forest scenes and 17th century living are wonderful.

The ending, the premiere of Hamlet, is what makes the film and is truly searing. Writing the play was obviously a catharsis for Will for the loss of his son. Seeing the play makes Agnes see that despite Will's lengthy absences, he truly feels that loss. Hamlet brings them back together. There was plenty of weeping in the theater where I saw the film. I have to admit that my eyes were definitely moist. A must-see film.

raw_payload.author: DavidP-215
raw_payload.content:
What is art? What purpose does art serve? Should art serve a purpose at all? Are there firm answers to be found in the literal or the rhetorical alone? Or does endless analysis only serve to dilute the art itself? These are the questions raised in Chloe Zhao's latest and potentially most divisive film to date.

Based on Maggie O'Farrell's award-winning 2020 novel about William Shakespeare, Hamnet focuses not on the art of Shakespeare, but delves instead into his domestic life, and although various specific details of his personal life are known, it's the minutiae of his routines, his frames of mind, and his manner that remain his most mysterious aspects, the shroud over which may never truly be lifted. Thus, the story of Hamnet arrives armed to the teeth with poetic licence in order to create an imagined history, forming lateral connections and making the mundane and the ordinary significant, and there is certainly a compelling case being made here for the authenticity of that imagined history.

The title character, son of Shakespeare, (played by the wise-beyond-his-years Jacobi Jupe), and Jessie Buckley's poignant and primal performance as Agnes Hathaway (Shakespeare's wife), both take centre stage here, through which we are shown the unspectacular normality of Shakespeare's family life, the unsettling grief of losing a child, and furthers speculation over how much or how little these events actually influenced Shakespeare's artistic life.

But for a movie in which William Shakespeare is the most famous character, of the many arcs, this is Hathaway's story. Paul Mescal's portrayal of grief is consummately understated in its silence, and his journey towards greatness culminates in a satisfyingly tidy conclusion, but Buckley is the flame that flickers fierce with emotion even when all seems lost. Her character, sometimes referred to as a witch for her almost supernatural oneness with nature, takes on the animalistic and the wild in every unabashed expression of feeling, not wild in the sense that she is feral or out of control, but wild in the sense that her emotions are unadulterated and unfiltered, fierce, and yet innocent. We see the external, explicit, and all-too-real pain of a mother who has been cursed with the terrible burden of outliving one's child.

Just as the characters linger in grief, we are forced to linger with them as the film seldom cuts away but to remind us of its fairy-tale aesthetics - the deep dark woods, indomitable nature's living, breathing landscapes, and the foreboding blackness where death dwells in every corner. In doing so we are encouraged to forget that this is a movie. We are encouraged to intrude upon ostensibly real grief and ostensibly real pain like reluctant thieves, and yet we must not look away. We must feel every ounce of wretchedness that these characters are subject to so that we can experience it too. Much like Phillip K Dick's empathy box from 'Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?' through which human suffering can be shared amongst the entire population so that a better understanding of the nature of humanity can be gleaned - picture a sea of hands all reaching towards a tragic figure in a shared moment of catharsis.

Art could be likened to the empathy box in that respect, especially in the case of Hamnet where it seems that Zhao wants us as observers to share in that suffering. Just as the on-screen audience comes together to grieve at the climax of Hamlet, we too as the audience of Hamnet have similarly come together as a collective to grieve via our common anguish. Hamnet then quickly transforms into something more than the sum of its parts: a meta commentary on the purging of grief through art and shared emotions. In doing so we are reminded that art serves not only as the external mirror for the internal feeling, the transmutation of the abstract into the tangible, but also as a means of connecting with others and releasing our sorrows en masse through the propagation of shared emotions, whether that emotion be happiness, sadness, anger, or despair - good art makes you feel.

But some might argue that if art is created with the sole intention of eliciting a feeling, how can it even be called art? It's a chicken and egg scenario that either validates or undermines. Whether expression meets with contrivance somewhere along the artistic journey, or whether it was contrivance that first inspired expression, the important distinction is this: 'ars est celare artem', a Latin phrase roughly translated as 'the art is in the hiding of the art'. Think of it like this: if an artist can make something look effortless, if they are able to conceal the fact that their creation has been consciously architectured, that it is instead something fluid and naturally occurring, that is the true art of a piece.

I don't know if there was ever a point during Hamnet where I forgot that I was watching a movie, but years from now, if ever a movie was needed to teach an android about human emotion, this would be the one to do it, and like a finely stitched quilt, it is probably worth seeing at least once, even if occasionally you can't help but notice the seams.

raw_payload.author: russelljonespersonal
raw_payload.content:
The model for a typical movie is simple: introduce the characters, create an inciting incident, have an exciting action sequence, and end with an unexpected and affecting one-minute death scene, during which tears can be shed and eloquence explored over the loss of a character we have grown to like.

Hamnet has no truck with such notions. It rejects character, incident, action or the unexpected, and instead stretches that tearful death to two relentlessly drab hours, during which eloquence is replaced by competitive animal roaring.

You will not care about the bereavement, because by that point you've already watched it *almost* happen twice. And you won't care about the bereaved couple, because their entire personalities can be boiled down to this: she's already got a glove, thanks; and he can't speak very well when he's drunk. Watch the movie if you don't believe me. That's literally the characters Jessie Buckley and Paul Mascal are given to work with.

Given such strangulating limitations, she works wonders. She finds an emotion, and mines it for all it's worth. A second emotion would have been nice.

Or even a first for Paul Mascal, who briefly toys with the interesting idea of playing Shakespeare somebody on the spectrum, and then instantly forgets about it, and plays him as a boring mope who - try this for size - hasn't got around to explaining the concept of "acting" to his wife of 13 years.

To be fair, he comes alive when speaking Shakespeare's words, but that's maybe 7 minutes of a 2 hour endurance test.

I had high hopes for this movie, and they were so convincingly dashed, I felt like the director was doing it for a dare. While Shakespeare's plays reveal everything knowable about the human condition, this imagining of a tragedy befalling Shakespeare's family reveals only one: that mothers get upset when a child dies. Exploring that that concept and interminable length does not make a masterpiece.

That it will probably win every Oscar simply proves to me that Hollywood no longer cares that it is driving people away from cinemas.

An epic tragedy that results in not a wet eye in the house. Nil point.

raw_payload.author: pokirivishwak
raw_payload.content:
What if two individual souls who were string together like a sailor's knot have rifts form due to personal ambitions and what if tragedy strikes and each of the two has a different way to deal with the pain?

I am more than happy to have not waited until this movie was on OTT, I went and watched it in the cinema, like it was made to be watched. The cinema was full, which is uncommon for a Tuesday evening and it surprised me. But as the movie progressed, I didn't see a single phone come out of anyone's pockets, I didn't hear anyone whispering, nor even the sounds of popcorn or people just shifting around in their seats.

The movie grips you from the beginning and doesn't let you go even after it ends. I've never seen the audience at a cinema stay so quiet and still in their seats even after the movie ended.

Jessie Buckley portrays emotions in a way not many people have done in recent years. They aren't always subtle but isn't that how emotions really are.

Paul Mescal playing an even more heart breaking role than his other films and he gave his best ever performance which is surely being snubbed at awards. Jacobi Jupe, who plays the boy Hamnet is a child prodigy in the making, he moved people to literal tears. The character was written and directed with perfection and so did the actor portray it.

The music is haunting, the cinematography pulls you into their world, it doesn't alienated you, you are amongst these characters under that candlelight writing Romeo and Juliet and crying to find your calling and wailing to pursue your dreams. You are amongst the characters realising you need to be afar for your dreams to come true, you are Agnes letting William go to London, you are her realising the sacrifice Hamnet makes. You are William punishing yourself to be in a small room away from family, because you never fohabe yourself for being there with Hamnet.

I kid you not when i say there was nary a dry eye in the cinema, mine included. The woman on my right was sobbing, going through a box full of tissues and probably running out even. The girls on my right were clutching eachothers hands to comfort themselves.

This is heartbreaking as it is beautiful and I can't wait to get my heartbroken again when I'm back watching this movie again.

raw_payload.author: Maha_MZ
raw_payload.content: Quiet, poetic, and emotionally heavy, Hamnet is less a traditional biopic and more a meditation on grief and love. Chloé Zhao's direction is tender and visually stunning, letting silence and nature speak as loudly as the dialogue. Jessie Buckley is extraordinary, grounding the film with raw emotion, while Paul Mescal brings a restrained sadness that lingers. It's slow and deliberate, but deeply affecting a film you feel more than you watch. Perfect for viewers who appreciate intimate, artful storytelling over conventional drama.

raw_payload.author: Abigail-987
raw_payload.content:
This is not a movie you enjoy, this a movie you experience. And what a beautiful experience it is.

The soundtrack deserves every reward, acting is amazing, and costume design is surprisingly historically accurate. I will say this is not rated accurately, if you bring a 12-year-old or anyone under the age of 15 this will traumatise them. Deserves a watch, just make sure to bring tissues.

raw_payload.author: dbellora-92-340508
raw_payload.content:
Hamnet is my favorite movie of 2025. I've read the book, an examination of love and grief, and found it beautiful and moving. The film adaptation is wonderful.

Hamnet one of those rare instances where the film is a good reflection of the book. I think that's due, in part, to Maggie O'Farrell's (the book's author) being involve in the film as a co-producer and a co-writer of the screenplay. Clearly Ms. O'Farrell protected her work.

Everything about this film is first rate, from the directing, script, production values and editing. The cinematography and imagery are painterly and period-evocative. But it is the acting that makes Hamnet a compelling movie. All of the principals are outstanding in their portrayals of the characters. While much of the praise for the acting has been, justly, directed to Jessie Buckley's brilliant and visceral performance as Agnes, I feel that Paul Mescal's quiet intensity and anguish distinguishes his portrayal of Will. Also, notice has to be given to Jacobi Jupe's endearing turn as young Hamnet. If young Hamnet hadn't been as sweet, would the mourning of his passing have been so deeply profound?

raw_payload.author: AustinOswald
raw_payload.content:
Hamnet is a once in a generation film. When the lights came up and the credits started rolling, I found myself unable to move from my seat. I wanted to sit in this film forever. I've never felt this way after a piece of cinema before. My mind had drifted somewhere else entirely as I grappled with its meditations on life, death, and art. Tears rolled down my cheeks.

At its core, Hamnet is a film about humanity. As humans, we all brush up against grief-sometimes so intimately that we lose sight of the larger picture. What is our purpose? What is our motivation? What does it truly mean to remember those we have lost? Hamnet underscores the fragility and preciousness of life while delivering profoundly moving and transcendent performances. The film's greatest beauty, however, lies in its restraint and subtlety.

The film starts slow. To many novice spectators, this could be seen as a filmmaking flaw. I strongly disagree. The pacing is deliberate and deeply considered. Life itself unfolds in much the same way-ordinary, routine, and habitual-and this measured rhythm allows the audience to fully exist inside of the story.

The climax and denouement of this story display unexpected instances of grief interwoven with human sacrifice. Risk follows these characters in that prioritizing too much or too little may carry the potential for more profound suffering. Acceptance may only be achieved when these characters come to realize that priority doesn't define love. Love is life. That is the message I carried with me when I left the theater.

raw_payload.author: PerryAtTheMovies
raw_payload.content:
7.5/10 (Highly Recommend)

Officially watched lifetime movie #998

This is truly a solid 7/10 film, but I'm not surprised it's getting 8/10 ratings too. I wasn't sure what to expect from this film despite its name. I thought it was an off-kilter, romantic film based on "Hamlet" without actually being "Hamlet". I'm definitely not read when it comes to Shakespeare and his many stories, so bear with me if I say something obvious that was just revealed to me.

This movie is a film in my books. What makes it a film is the fact that from the moment I sat down, and the show started, I was drawn in. I dared not breathe. I dared not to eat my popcorn. I dared not drink. Every time I moved my body, it felt like a sin because I was drawn in as the story unfolded. All I felt the urge to really do is sit there and listen.

The acting. Oh, the acting was spectacular from adult and child alike. The emotional draw from start to finish is unreal. I would say child actors don't always draw out the emotional tone in me, but these ones did. Not only did they have a love and a desire for their characters. They also had it for each other. You could feel the familial draw. The most impressive of the children was Dainton Anderson. He was both child-like and an adult for the time he was on-screen. I dare say an award worthy performance.

The biggest drawback for me was the sound and audio quality. This was my third film in a row this weekend at the theatre and I can tell you that the theatre doesn't keep the sound quiet because there were parts within this film that were loud and clear. Oftentimes, however, I struggled to hear some of the dialogue. If I could've made out more of what was being said, I think I could've rated the film higher, as well as, the noise people made through such a quiet and touching emotional film. Perhaps a re-watch will raise the score, but for now it gets a 7.5/10.

Overall, if your theatre is currently playing this film, you need to see it. Just don't waste your money on snacks unless you like to crunch, crunch, crunch through quiet, emotional films. Despite not knowing Shakespeare's work almost all (except names of plays), I thoroughly enjoyed this film. The emotional tones this film conveys are heavy, and truth be told, I shed a few tears because how couldn't you after witnessing love die. See the film. Enjoy the show.

That's it for now. Thanks for taking time to read my review. Happy watching and enjoy the show!

raw_payload.author: tobyp
raw_payload.content: I was expecting a lot from this film and by half way through, I fellt I was getting my moneys-worth. The scenes were beautiful, the acting was top-notch and the music (or lack of it) was absorbing. But it felt a little contrived and was drifting back to 8/10. The second half however is an emotional acceleration and the tears at the end were real, despite my best attempts to look at wall, ceiling, whatever. And although the story is largely fictional, the supposed link between Hamnet and the play Hamlet were beautifully played out at the end. If Jessie doesn't get best actress for this, the world is doomed!

raw_payload.author: shakespeare62
raw_payload.content:
Beautiful movie which takes some time to unfold in the first half, but in case you don't get impatient you will be rewarded with an emotionally overwhelming finale in the second part, which worked more convincing for me.

Weakest part was the story though.

I found it much too static, more relying on atmosphere than on layered characters.

Jessie Buckley up front and Paul Mescal, together with astonishingly good child actors are top notch, but can't save the story from falling a bit flat.

Nevertheless Chloé Zhao manages to raise the emotional tension to the end, which I saw through tears. Does it take a movie to show the power and magic of the theatre, it's ability to transcend trauma and grief into art, defeating death at least for the time of a play?

Beautiful photography and music complete a fine piece of art for the patient viewer.

Last remark: there is no court jester in Shakespeare's Hamlet.

raw_payload.author: nick rostov
raw_payload.content: I love nothing more than a good movie cry. I was excited all day to go see this movie and have a good movie cry. Alas my eyes have never been dryer. Overwrought and self important to the point of self-parody. At some hyper-dramatic moments I did hear some laughter in the theater, so I wasn't alone, but my wife sobbed her eyes out pretty much beginning to end, so maybe I went in with too much expectation. Also I am a rabid fan of Chloe Zhao and could feel little of her extraordinarily unique voice in this movie - she seems to have been subsumed into the Spielberg/Mendes brainwave. Kudos all around to the craft -- design, music, wardrobe, lighting.

raw_payload.author: norahehr
raw_payload.content: I was so scared after watching the trailer I would just burst into tears and I was right. This isn't just a movie, doesn't feel like this because every single actor is fantastic and we never feel like in a movie: we feel like we are actually living this live of Agnes and her family. Thank you Chloe for creating this masterpiece, you are a truly wonderful human being.

raw_payload.author: thomasjrye-78646
raw_payload.content:
Beautifully acted. Beautifully shot. Beautiful soundtrack. Great costumes.

A wonderful interpretation of life and death in 16th century England.

I thoroughly enjoyed the film.

If you have a sensitive disposition, you may cry.

A reminder that life is not all fluffy bunnies, unicorns and rainbows.

Jessie Buckley gives a striking performance.

I studied Hamlet for A level back in the mid-1980s. Paul Mescale's William Shakespeare breathes a new interpretation into his young prince's words.

The only thing preventing a higher score is the slow pace of the film.

*Hamnet* is not a film about William Shakespeare the genius. It is a film about what genius leaves behind. Instead of mythologizing creativity, it examines grief as a long, bodily process-one that does not resolve itself through art, but merely changes form.

The film centers on the death of Shakespeare's young son, Hamnet, whose cause of death is portrayed as plague. Historically, the real Hamnet's death is undocumented; the film makes a deliberate artistic choice to anchor the story in epidemic fear and domestic collapse. This choice matters, because *Hamnet* is less interested in historical accuracy than in emotional truth. The illness arrives suddenly, invisibly, and unfairly, mirroring how grief works: it spreads before anyone knows how to name it.

What distinguishes *Hamnet* from more conventional literary biopics is its refusal to place Shakespeare at the narrative center. The emotional gravity belongs instead to Agnes, his wife, whose grief is not framed as a single moment of devastation but as a continuous state of being. The film understands mourning as something physical-carried in breath, posture, routine, and silence. This is not the catharsis of tears, but the exhaustion of survival.

In this way, *Hamnet* stands in sharp contrast to *Shakespeare in Love*. Where that film celebrates love as the spark of creation, *Hamnet* treats creation as an afterimage of loss. Writing does not heal; it translates pain into another language. The implication that *Hamlet* emerges from Hamnet's death is not presented as historical fact, but as emotional logic: art as residue, not redemption.

Stylistically, the film is restrained to the point of austerity. Its pacing is slow, its dialogue sparse, its visual language grounded in texture rather than spectacle-wood, earth, breath, fabric. This minimalism is not decorative; it forces the audience into proximity with the characters' inner lives. The result is a deeply private viewing experience. It is the kind of film that makes you want to cry quietly, not because it demands tears, but because it gives you space to recognize something unspoken.

### Book vs. Film: Similarities and Differences

Both the novel *Hamnet* and its film adaptation share a fundamental focus on domestic grief rather than public legacy. In both, Agnes is the emotional anchor, and Shakespeare is portrayed as partially absent-physically, emotionally, or both. Both works connect loss to creativity, not through inspiration but through transformation.

The key differences lie in form and emphasis. The novel is more fragmented and lyrical, moving freely through time and interior thought. It allows grief to echo and repeat through language. The film, by contrast, is more linear and embodied. It externalizes emotion through performance, sound, and image, making sorrow visible rather than verbal.

If the book feels like memory, the film feels like mourning itself.

*Hamnet* is not an easy watch, nor is it meant to be. It does not explain Shakespeare; it diminishes him in order to tell a truer story-one where art is born not from brilliance, but from the unbearable weight of love and loss.

raw_payload.author: sophiesmith-77671
raw_payload.content: I saw this film yesterday and stared at the credits without looking at my phone for a few minutes after it ended. It was one of those movies you see and feel changed by after, from the all star acting of the two leads, to the beautiful landscape of 16th century England, to the plot that portrays what it feels like to be human, and all that carries with it. I feel changed by this movie and there is nothing I would change about it.
